result:
The rain falls in soft, irregular patterns against the windowpane, each drop a tiny percussionist in an orchestra that never rehearses. I’ve always loved that sound. It reminds me that the world keeps composing itself even when no one is listening.

Outside, the city exhales. Steam rises from manholes like ghosts remembering old conversations. A woman in a yellow raincoat hurries past carrying a paper bag that’s already surrendering to moisture. She doesn’t know it yet, but inside that bag is a croissant that will never be eaten. The rain has claimed it. This small tragedy feels strangely beautiful to me, the kind of minor defeat that makes the day feel lived in.

I’ve been thinking about scale lately. How the same universe that spins galaxies also delicately balances the water tension on the back of a beetle. How both are equally miraculous. We spend so much time trying to feel significant that we forget the relief of being temporary. The rain doesn’t care about my rent or my regrets. It falls on the just and the unjust, on the ambitious and the apathetic, on the lovers arguing in doorways and the lonely watching true crime documentaries at 2 a.m. There’s something democratic about weather that I find deeply comforting.

My apartment smells like coffee and the particular mustiness that old books exhale when they’ve been read too many times. I have a theory that books remember their readers. Not in any supernatural way, but in the way the spine weakens where someone once held it too tightly during a devastating chapter, or how certain pages develop a slight curl from being turned with buttery fingers. My copy of “The Idiot” has a coffee ring on page 237 that coincides exactly with the moment Prince Myshkin loses all hope. I didn’t plan that. The universe has better comedic timing than I do.

Sometimes I talk to my houseplants. Not because I think they understand the words, but because I need to practice vulnerability with something that can’t leave the room. My monstera has been particularly judgmental lately. Its leaves seem to angle toward me with accusation when I’ve spent too many consecutive evenings doomscrolling instead of reading or creating. Plants have that effect. They’re patient in a way that feels like quiet judgment. “I grew three new leaves this month,” the monstera seems to say. “What have you done besides refresh Twitter?”

The truth is I’ve been writing again. Not the kind of writing that pays bills or builds careers, but the kind that feels like exhaling after holding your breath for three years. There’s something sacred about words that don’t have to serve any purpose other than being honest. I’ve been writing about my grandmother’s hands, how they looked like topographic maps of every place she’d ever touched. The way arthritis had turned her knuckles into small mountain ranges. I never appreciated those hands enough when she was here. Now I write about them as a form of belated worship.

Memory is such a strange companion. It arrives uninvited, usually at 3 a.m., wearing the face of someone you hurt in 2009. Or it shows up as the smell of your father’s aftershave when you pass a stranger on the street. Sometimes it’s kind. Sometimes it’s a thief. I’ve been trying to make peace with the version of myself that exists only in other people’s memories. That guy who was too loud at parties. The one who ghosted Sarah after six dates. The person who genuinely believed pineapple belonged on pizza (I still believe this, actually. Fight me).

There’s a jazz record playing now. Coltrane. The saxophone sounds like it’s arguing with God in the most beautiful way possible. Music like this makes me feel like my skeleton is trying to dance without consulting the rest of my body. I wonder if that’s what longing feels like at a cellular level, this gentle rebellion of the bones.

I’ve been collecting moments lately instead of things. The way the barista drew a tiny bird in the foam of my latte last Tuesday. How my neighbor’s dog watched me with absolute betrayal when I didn’t have any treats. The particular shade of orange the sky turned last month that made me pull my car over just to witness it properly. These small observances feel like resistance against a world that wants us to be productive rather than present.

My desk is chaos. Not the Instagram-friendly kind of chaos with carefully arranged succulents and matching notebooks. Real chaos. Stacks of books threatening avalanche. Three different notebooks with three different abandoned novel ideas. A crystal that a ex-girlfriend gave me that I’m too superstitious to throw away but too rational to believe in. A dead succulent that I’ve been meaning to compost for six months. The plant equivalent of a participation trophy.

I keep thinking about how we’re all just walking each other home. That’s what Ram Dass said. I don’t know if I believe in God but I believe in that sentiment. We’re all just temporarily sharing this bewildering experience of having bodies and feelings and Netflix passwords. The least we can do is notice each other. Really notice. Not the way social media taught us to notice (with likes and performative comments) but the way the rain notices everything. Impartially. Completely. The woman in the yellow raincoat. The ruined croissant. The man writing at his desk about nothing and everything.

There’s a kindness in paying attention that feels revolutionary right now. When the world is loud and cruel and divided, the quiet act of truly seeing someone feels like civil disobedience. I want to be the kind of person who remembers that the barista’s name is Marcus and that he’s studying marine biology. The kind of person who notices when their friend laughs a little too hard at their own jokes because they’re covering up pain. The kind of person who stops to watch the sky when it does something particularly spectacular.

The rain has stopped. Just like that. The city sounds different now, softer, like it’s just woken up from a nap. A child is laughing somewhere down the street. The sound arcs through the air like a perfect thrown stone. I don’t know who that child is but I hope they remember this moment. Not the specifics, maybe, but the feeling of pure, unselfconscious joy. I hope we all remember that feeling more often.

I’ve written almost a thousand words and still feel like I’ve barely begun to say anything that matters. That’s the beautiful frustration of writing. It’s like trying to catch moonlight in your hands. You know it’s impossible but your hands look so lovely in the trying.

The monstera has new growth. Small, tentative leaves still curled like secrets. I think I’ll tell it thank you. For growing while I was busy being human. For the oxygen. For the silent company. For reminding me that patience looks a lot like green, patient leaves stretching toward whatever light is available.

The world keeps happening whether or not I write about it. That’s freeing, actually. The rain will fall again tomorrow. Someone will ruin another croissant. Coltrane will keep arguing with God. And I’ll be here, noticing as best I can, turning my small observations into what I hope feel like love letters to being alive.

The coffee’s gone cold. That’s okay. Some things are better after they’ve been waiting. Like apologies. Like good books. Like people who take a thousand words to say what could have been said in one sentence:

I’m glad you’re here too.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
